figures.gloss: The central ritual of modern Heathenry, involving the consecration and offering of mead, ale, or food to the gods, ancestors, or land spirits. It structurally enacts the ancient Germanic concept of 'do ut des'—a gift for a gift.
figures.facet: It categorically rejects Christian models of atonement or submission. The offering is a mechanism of hospitality and mutual obligation among equals or kin, building a shared 'luck' economy.
figures.provenance: Reconstructed from medieval sagas (e.g., Heimskringla, Hakon the Good) and codified in the 1970s by Sveinbjörn Beinteinsson and the Ásatrúarfélagið.

figures.gloss: The interconnected web of causality and destiny. Wyrd represents the grand, unfolding tapestry of the cosmos, while Örlög constitutes the specific, accumulated layers of past actions—both ancestral and personal—that shape present conditions.
figures.facet: Unlike passive fatalism, it is highly active. A Heathen is not merely bound by the past; their conscious deeds in the present weave new layers into the well, actively reshaping future Örlög.
figures.provenance: Old English poetry (Beowulf, The Wanderer) and Old Norse eddas (Völuspá); popularized as a central theological tenet by Paul Bauschatz's 1982 work 'The Well and the Tree'.

figures.gloss: A formalized ritual drinking round consisting of toasts, boasts, and oaths spoken over a horn of mead. Typically divided into rounds honoring deities, ancestors, and personal achievements.
figures.facet: Words spoken over the horn are believed to directly alter reality, dropping into the well of Wyrd. A broken oath at sumbel is viewed not just as a social faux pas, but a metaphysical injury.
figures.provenance: Beowulf, Lokasenna, and various sagas; structured into three specific rounds by modern American Heathen groups in the 1970s and 1980s.

figures.gloss: A list of nine ethical maxims—Courage, Truth, Honor, Fidelity, Discipline, Hospitality, Industriousness, Self-Reliance, and Perseverance—intended to summarize a pre-Christian Germanic worldview for modern practitioners.
figures.facet: It highlights the modern craving for systematized morality. Highly controversial today, it is defended by some as a necessary framework, while criticized by strict reconstructionists as a Victorian or overly Protestant codification.
figures.provenance: Created in the 1970s by John Yeowell of the Odinic Rite and Edred Thorsson (Stephen Flowers) of the Asatru Free Assembly.

figures.gloss: The wights or spirits inherent to the natural landscape, rocks, rivers, and trees. They are the immediate, local guardians of the physical environment, demanding respect from anyone who treads or builds on their land.
figures.facet: A modern Heathen might have a distant relationship with the major gods but maintains strict, regular ritual diplomacy with their backyard land-wight to ensure peace and agricultural or domestic success.
figures.provenance: Landnámabók (the Book of Settlements) and Egil's Saga; central to Icelandic law prohibiting dragon-prows from frightening local spirits.

figures.gloss: The Allfather, god of poetry, frenzy, runes, and esoteric wisdom. Modern Heathenry emphasizes his role as a relentless wanderer who sacrifices himself to himself on the World Tree to grasp the secrets of existence.
figures.facet: He is venerated less as an omnipotent, benevolent patriarch and more as a model of relentless self-overcoming and dangerous spiritual initiation. His wisdom is deeply tied to ecstasy and boundary-breaking.
figures.provenance: The Poetic Edda (Hávamál, Völuspá) and Prose Edda; heavily emphasized by Odinic and runic revivalists of the 20th century.

figures.gloss: The red-bearded god of thunder, agriculture, and the protection of humanity. He is the warder of Midgard, actively wielding his hammer Mjölnir against chaotic forces that threaten the cosmic or social order.
figures.facet: He is the democratic, accessible counterweight to Odin's aristocratic elitism. Modern practitioners invoke him for everyday physical strength, defense of the marginalized, and the blessing of marriages and homes.
figures.provenance: The Poetic Edda (Þrymskviða) and widespread archaeological presence of Mjölnir amulets across the Germanic world, adopted today as the primary symbol of the faith.

edges.note: Intra-tradition: Blót establishes the relationship with the divine through physical sacrifice, while Sumbel solidifies the social and spiritual bonds of the community through sacred speech.
Intra-tradition: Words spoken into the horn during Sumbel are explicitly believed to drop into the well of Örlög, permanently weaving the speaker's oaths into the web of Wyrd.
Intra-tradition: They form a complementary dyad of the aristocratic, esoteric seeker (Odin) and the pragmatic, accessible guardian of the common folk (Thor).
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: Modern Odinism bridges the practitioner to the ancient mythic axis; Odin's ritual sacrifice on Yggdrasil serves as the ultimate template for shamanic initiation.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: Modern Heathenry directly revitalizes the historic Norse Thor, shifting him from a mythic relic to a living patron of the working class and community defense.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: Both represent a fundamental, underlying cosmic matrix of cause, effect, and truthful action that even deities are subject to.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: Both represent an underlying cosmic and moral order that humans must actively maintain through correct speech, ritual, and harmonious living.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: Both are wandering, gray-bearded archetypes of esoteric knowledge who wield profound magical power through poetry and song rather than brute force.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: A classic Dumézilian parallel where both are sovereign, magically-binding sky gods whose authority rests on occult knowledge and fetters rather than martial combat.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: Both are Indo-European thunderers associated with a specific symbolic weapon (hammer and wheel) and invoked for the protection of the tribe.

Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: Both traditions venerate localized, territorial nature spirits that directly control the fertility and safety of the land, requiring active appeasement by local inhabitants.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: Both integrate the veneration of the active, mighty dead directly into living community ritual, relying on spoken lineage and toasts or offerings.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: Both feature outdoor, pragmatic rituals centered on offering a sacred food or drink directly to deities and natural forces to secure agricultural and communal luck.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: Both revolve around cyclical community feasts where humans and gods share the yield of the earth, reinforcing cosmic order through reciprocal hospitality.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: Both represent attempts to codify and systematize an indigenous polytheistic worldview into a concrete framework for correct behavior and ritual procedure.

Class seam of European reconstructionism: communal open-air offering rites rebuilt from medieval sources and archaeology on the same gift-for-a-gift logic, in movements that arose independently but in parallel.

Class seam of European reconstructionism: each movement's modern systematization of an order-concept from old sources — darna as harmony to be maintained, wyrd as the web one weaves into — parallel constructions, no contact required.

Class seam of European reconstructionism: offering rites rebuilt from period sources on the reciprocal gift-logic — libation and khernips beside mead and horn — parallel modern reconstructions, not contact.

Class seam of modern reconstructionism: offering rites rebuilt from ancient sources on a reciprocal logic — senut's reversion of offerings beside the blót's gift-for-a-gift — parallel modern constructions from unrelated ancient models.

dossier.essence: The modern revival and reconstruction of the pre-Christian Germanic and Norse religion, emerging in the early 1970s — organized in Iceland as the Ásatrúarfélagið (founded 1972, its first allsherjargoði the poet Sveinbjörn Beinteinsson) and in Britain and North America under names such as Ásatrú, Odinism, and Heathenry. Practitioners worship the Norse gods — the Æsir and Vanir, including Odin, Thor, and Freyja — honor ancestors and land-spirits, and rebuild their religion from medieval texts and archaeology.
dossier.cosmos: Drawn from Norse myth as preserved in the Eddas: nine worlds arrayed on the world-tree Yggdrasil, from Asgard of the gods through Midgard of humans to Hel of the dead, framed between the shaping of the world from the giant Ymir and its destruction and renewal at Ragnarök. Fate is central — the web of wyrd and örlög laid down at the well of the Norns, which even the gods must meet, and which many modern Heathens hold is actively re-woven by present deeds. Afterlife beliefs (Valhalla, Hel, rebirth in the family line) are various and loosely held.
dossier.practice: The two core rites are the blót — an offering of mead, ale, or food to gods, ancestors, and land-wights on the reciprocal principle of 'a gift for a gift' — and the sumbel, a ritual round of toasts, boasts, and oaths over the horn whose spoken words are held to drop into the well of wyrd. Practice is decentralized among households, kindreds, and larger fellowships, led by elected or informal goðar rather than a priesthood; widely cited values include hospitality, kin-peace (frith), and the modern, contested 'Nine Noble Virtues.'
dossier.canon: No scripture; the sources are the medieval Icelandic texts — the 'Poetic Edda' (especially Völuspá and Hávamál), Snorri Sturluson's 'Prose Edda' (c. 1220) and 'Heimskringla,' the Icelandic sagas, and Old English poetry such as 'Beowulf' — read alongside archaeology and place-name evidence.
dossier.reflects: The reconstructionist impulse — a religion deliberately rebuilt, and honest that its own scriptures were written down by Christians centuries after conversion — foregrounding reciprocity and kin-bound honor over salvation or submission.
sources: Jennifer Snook, 'American Heathens: The Politics of Identity in a Pagan Religious Movement' (2015)
Stefanie von Schnurbein, 'Norse Revival: Transformations of Germanic Neopaganism' (2016)
Michael F. Strmiska (ed.), 'Modern Paganism in World Cultures' (2005)
Jeffrey Kaplan, 'Radical Religion in America: Millenarian Movements from the Far Right to the Children of Noah' (1997)
